docs.title: Q1. Maximum Cluster Quality
docs.text:
Topic: greedy, heap, sorting, priority-queue

When building a computing cluster consisting of several machines, two parameters are most important: speed and reliability. The quality of a computing cluster is the sum of its machines' speeds multiplied by the minimum reliability of its machines.

Given information about several available machines, select machines to create a cluster of less than or equal to a particular size. Determine the maximum quality of cluster that can be created.

docs.title: Function Description
docs.text:
Complete the function maxClusterQuality in the editor below.

maxClusterQuality has the following parameter(s): * int speed[n]: an integer array of size \(n\), such that speed[i] is the speed of the \(i\)-th machine * int reliability[n]: an integer array of size \(n\), such that reliability[i] is the reliability of the \(i\)-th machine * int maxMachines: an integer denoting the maximum number of machines you want in a cluster

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: • long long (or int depending on arbitrary precision in Python): the maximum quality of a computing cluster that can be built.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• \(1 \le n \le 10^5\)
  • \(1 \le speed[i] \le 10^5\)
  • \(1 \le reliability[i] \le 10^5\)
  • \(1 \le maxMachines \le n\)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
n = 5
speed = [4, 3, 15, 5, 6]
reliability = [7, 6, 1, 2, 8]
maxMachines = 3
The maximum number of machines to use is maxMachines = 3 chosen from n = 5 available machines. Select the first, second, and fifth machines. The quality of the cluster is: (speed[0] + speed[1] + speed[4]) * min(reliability[0], reliability[1], reliability[4]) = (4 + 3 + 6) * min(7, 6, 8) = 13 * 6 = 78. This is the highest quality that can be achieved, so the answer is 78.

docs.title: Sample Case 0
docs.text:
Sample Input 

5 -> speed[] size n = 5
12 -> speed[] = [12, 112, 100, 13, 55]
112
100
13
55
5 -> reliability[] size n = 5
31 -> reliability[] = [31, 4, 100, 55, 50]
4
100
55
50
3 -> maxMachines = 3
Sample Output 
10000
Explanation There are 5 machines available. Their speeds are 12, 112, 100, 13, and 55 respectively, while their reliabilities are 31, 4, 100, 55, and 50 respectively. The maximum number of machines allowed in a cluster is 3. The best quality of a cluster can be achieved by selecting only the third machine. The quality of a cluster will be 100 * 100 = 10000.

docs.title: Python 3 Solution
docs.text:
import heapq

def maxClusterQuality(speed, reliability, maxMachines):
    # Pair each machine's speed and reliability and sort by reliability in descending order
    machines = sorted(zip(reliability, speed), key=lambda x: x[0], reverse=True)

    max_quality = 0
    speed_sum = 0
    min_heap = []

    for rel, spd in machines:
        heapq.heappush(min_heap, spd)
        speed_sum += spd

        # If the size of the cluster exceeds maxMachines, remove the machine with the smallest speed
        if len(min_heap) > maxMachines:
            speed_sum -= heapq.heappop(min_heap)

        # The current reliability is the minimum reliability of all machines currently in the cluster
        max_quality = max(max_quality, speed_sum * rel)

    return max_quality

docs.title: C++ Solution
docs.text:
#include <vector>
#include <queue>
#include <algorithm>

using namespace std;

long long maxClusterQuality(vector<int>& speed, vector<int>& reliability, int maxMachines) {
    int n = speed.size();
    vector<pair<int, int>> machines(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        machines[i] = {reliability[i], speed[i]};
    }

    // Sort machines by reliability in descending order
    sort(machines.rbegin(), machines.rend());

    priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> min_heap;
    long long speed_sum = 0;
    long long max_quality = 0;

    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        int rel = machines[i].first;
        int spd = machines[i].second;

        min_heap.push(spd);
        speed_sum += spd;

        if (min_heap.size() > maxMachines) {
            speed_sum -= min_heap.top();
            min_heap.pop();
        }

        max_quality = max(max_quality, speed_sum * rel);
    }

    return max_quality;
}

docs.title: Q2. Minimum Leaves to Remove
docs.text:
Topic: trees, dfs, graphs, depth-first-search

The country of Hackerland can be represented as a tree of tree_nodes nodes numbered from 1 to tree_nodes. The \(i\)-th edge is a bidirectional connection between the nodes numbered tree_from[i] and tree_to[i] and has a weight tree_weight[i]. Each node \(i\) is associated with an integer arr[i]. The tree is rooted at node index 1.

A tree is special if for every node \(u\) and every ancestor \(a\) of \(u\) in the tree, we have: $\(arr[u] \ge distance(a, u)\)$ where \(distance(a, u)\) is the sum of weights of the edges on the unique path between nodes \(a\) and \(u\).

A leaf of the tree is a node connected to a single node by a single edge. In one operation, any one leaf is removed from the tree. The root of the tree cannot be removed. Find the minimum number of leaves that must be removed in order to make the tree special. (Note: Removing a node also removes its connection, potentially making its parent a leaf, which can then be removed in subsequent operations).

docs.title: Function Description
docs.text:
Complete the function getMinLeavesToRemove in the editor below.

getMinLeavesToRemove has the following parameters: * int tree_nodes: the number of nodes * int tree_from[tree_edges]: one end of each edge * int tree_to[tree_edges]: the other end of each edge * int tree_weight[tree_edges]: edge weights * arr[tree_nodes]: an array of integers (1-indexed node values)

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: • int: the minimum number of leaves that need to be removed from the tree to make it special.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• \(1 \le tree\_nodes \le 10^5\)
  • tree_edges = tree_nodes - 1
  • The graph is connected
  • \(1 \le tree\_from[i], tree\_to[i] \le tree\_nodes\)
  • \(-10^9 \le tree\_weight[i] \le 10^9\)
  • \(1 \le arr[i] \le 10^9\)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
tree_nodes = 5
arr = [12, 2, 27, 11, 1]
tree_from = [1, 1, 3, 3]
tree_to = [2, 3, 4, 5]
tree_weight = [8, 5, 2, 7]
* Node labels are <node number>:<arr[node number]>. * Node 1: The root is not removed. * Node 2: Test arr[2] > dist(node 2, node 1), i.e. \(2 > 8\) is false, so remove the node. * Node 3: Test arr[3] > dist(node 3, node 1), i.e. \(27 > 5\) is true, so the node remains. * Node 4: Test 11 > 2 and 11 > 2 + 5. Both are true, so node remains. * Node 5: Test 1 > 7. This is false, so remove node 5.

The minimum number of leaves that must be removed is 2 (nodes 2 and 5).

docs.title: Python 3 Solution
docs.text:
import sys
# Increase recursion depth for deep trees
sys.setrecursionlimit(300000)

def getMinLeavesToRemove(tree_nodes, tree_from, tree_to, tree_weight, arr):
    # Build adjacency list
    adj = [[] for _ in range(tree_nodes + 1)]
    for i in range(len(tree_from)):
        u = tree_from[i]
        v = tree_to[i]
        w = tree_weight[i]
        adj[u].append((v, w))
        adj[v].append((u, w))

    # Calculate subtree sizes
    subtree_size = [0] * (tree_nodes + 1)

    def calc_subtree_size(u, p):
        size = 1
        for v, w in adj[u]:
            if v != p:
                size += calc_subtree_size(v, u)
        subtree_size[u] = size
        return size

    calc_subtree_size(1, 0)

    # DFS to count the removed nodes
    def dfs(u, p, current_depth, min_ancestor_depth):
        # Root node (1) cannot be removed and has no ancestors
        if u != 1:
            # Check if there is some ancestor that makes this node bad
            if current_depth - min_ancestor_depth > arr[u - 1]:
                # If u is bad, we must remove it and its entire subtree
                return subtree_size[u]

        removed_count = 0
        new_min_ancestor = min(min_ancestor_depth, current_depth)
        for v, w in adj[u]:
            if v != p:
                removed_count += dfs(v, u, current_depth + w, new_min_ancestor)
        return removed_count

    return dfs(1, 0, 0, float('inf'))

docs.title: C++ Solution
docs.text:
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <climits>

using namespace std;

struct Edge {
    int to;
    long long weight;
};

void calcSubtreeSize(int u, int p, const vector<vector<Edge>>& adj, vector<int>& subtree_size) {
    subtree_size[u] = 1;
    for (auto& edge : adj[u]) {
        if (edge.to != p) {
            calcSubtreeSize(edge.to, u, adj, subtree_size);
            subtree_size[u] += subtree_size[edge.to];
        }
    }
}

int dfs(int u, int p, long long current_depth, long long min_ancestor_depth,
        const vector<vector<Edge>>& adj, const vector<int>& subtree_size, const vector<int>& arr) {
    if (u != 1) {
        if (current_depth - min_ancestor_depth > arr[u - 1]) {
            return subtree_size[u];
        }
    }

    int removed_count = 0;
    long long new_min_ancestor = min(min_ancestor_depth, current_depth);
    for (auto& edge : adj[u]) {
        if (edge.to != p) {
            removed_count += dfs(edge.to, u, current_depth + edge.weight, new_min_ancestor, adj, subtree_size, arr);
        }
    }
    return removed_count;
}

int getMinLeavesToRemove(int tree_nodes, vector<int>& tree_from, vector<int>& tree_to, vector<int>& tree_weight, vector<int>& arr) {
    vector<vector<Edge>> adj(tree_nodes + 1);
    for (size_t i = 0; i < tree_from.size(); ++i) {
        int u = tree_from[i];
        int v = tree_to[i];
        long long w = tree_weight[i];
        adj[u].push_back({v, w});
        adj[v].push_back({u, w});
    }

    vector<int> subtree_size(tree_nodes + 1, 0);
    calcSubtreeSize(1, 0, adj, subtree_size);

    return dfs(1, 0, 0LL, LLONG_MAX, adj, subtree_size, arr);
}

docs.title: Q1. Maximum balls (Exclude One Element to Maximize GCD)
docs.text:
Topic: Arrays, Number Theory, Prefix & Suffix Arrays, Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)

Bob is a math teacher with a class of N students. The marks obtained by the students are represented by the array marks. Bob wants to gift every student an equal number of balls. To do this, he needs to choose a number that is a factor of the marks of all N students.

In order to distribute the maximum number of balls, Bob can change the marks of at most one student to any positive number of his choice. Find the maximum number of balls Bob can give to every student after changing the marks of at most one student.

docs.title: Function description
docs.text: Complete the max_balls function. This function takes the following 2 parameters and returns an integer that represents the answer: - N: Represents the number of students in the class - marks: Represents the marks of N students

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• \(2 \le N \le 10^5\)
  • \(1 \le marks_i \le 10^9\)

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Input: - N: 3 - marks: [12, 3, 11] Output: 3 Explanation: - To distribute the maximum number of balls, Bob can change the marks of the 3rd student (with marks 11) to 39. - The array becomes [12, 3, 39], and the GCD of the marks is gcd(12, 3, 39) = 3. - Thus, Bob can give 3 balls to every student.

docs.title: Solution Explanation
docs.text:
Changing one element to any positive number of our choice means we can choose it to be a multiple of the GCD of all the remaining elements in the array. This effectively eliminates that element's constraint on the overall GCD. Thus, the problem reduces to finding the maximum GCD of any \(N-1\) elements in the array.

We can compute this efficiently in \(O(N)\) time by maintaining: 1. A prefix GCD array pref where pref[i] = gcd(marks[0], ..., marks[i]). 2. A suffix GCD array suff where suff[i] = gcd(marks[i], ..., marks[N-1]).

For each index i, the GCD of the remaining elements is: - If \(i = 0\): suff[1] - If \(i = N-1\): pref[N-2] - Otherwise: gcd(pref[i-1], suff[i+1])

We take the maximum value over all i.

import math

def max_balls(N: int, marks: list[int]) -> int:
    if N < 2:
        return 0

    # Build prefix GCDs
    pref = [0] * N
    pref[0] = marks[0]
    for i in range(1, N):
        pref[i] = math.gcd(pref[i-1], marks[i])

    # Build suffix GCDs
    suff = [0] * N
    suff[N-1] = marks[N-1]
    for i in range(N-2, -1, -1):
        suff[i] = math.gcd(suff[i+1], marks[i])

    max_gcd = 0
    for i in range(N):
        if i == 0:
            current_gcd = suff[1]
        elif i == N-1:
            current_gcd = pref[N-2]
        else:
            current_gcd = math.gcd(pref[i-1], suff[i+1])
        max_gcd = max(max_gcd, current_gcd)

    return max_gcd
  • Time Complexity: \(O(N \log(\max(marks)))\) because we perform a constant number of \(O(\log(\text{value}))\) GCD operations per element.
  • Space Complexity: \(O(N)\) to store prefix and suffix arrays.

docs.title: Q1. String Program (minRemoval)
docs.text:
Topic: strings, greedy

Alice is working on a system that processes text inputs from users. One of the requirements is that the text must not contain the substring "ppp" - three consecutive 'p' characters. If this substring appears, the text is rejected.

To make the input text acceptable, Alice needs to remove characters in such a way that the substring "ppp" does not exist.

Your task is to help Alice find and return an integer value representing the minimum number of characters that need to be removed from the text to meet this requirement.

Note: * The string only contains lowercase alphabetic characters. * You can delete characters in random positions (not necessarily consecutive).

docs.title: Input Specification
docs.text: • input1: A string S, representing the text inputs from users.

docs.title: Output Specification
docs.text: • Return an integer value representing the minimum number of characters that need to be removed from the text to meet the above requirement.

docs.title: Example 1
docs.text:
• Input: pppiii
  • Output: 1
  • Explanation: Here the string S is "pppiii". Since the string contains three consecutive p's or the substring 'ppp', if the first character is removed from the string, then it becomes 'ppiii'. Now after removing 1 character, no such substring is there in the input text, so it will be accepted. Hence, 1 is returned as the output.

docs.title: Example 2
docs.text:
• Input: pppp
  • Output: 2
  • Explanation: Here the string S is "pppp". Since the string contains three consecutive p's or the substring 'ppp', if we remove the first character from the string then it becomes 'ppp'. Again, it contains substring 'ppp', so again the first character is removed. Now the string becomes 'pp'. After removing 2 characters from string, no such substring is there in the input text, so it will be accepted. Hence, 2 is returned as the output.

docs.title: Python 3 Solution
docs.text:
def minRemoval(input1: str) -> int:
    ans = 0
    count = 0
    for char in input1:
        if char == 'p':
            count += 1
        else:
            if count >= 3:
                ans += count - 2
            count = 0
    if count >= 3:
        ans += count - 2
    return ans

docs.title: Java Solution
docs.text:
public class UserMainCode {
    public int minRemoval(String input1) {
        int ans = 0;
        int count = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < input1.length(); i++) {
            char c = input1.charAt(i);
            if (c == 'p') {
                count++;
            } else {
                if (count >= 3) {
                    ans += (count - 2);
                }
                count = 0;
            }
        }
        if (count >= 3) {
            ans += (count - 2);
        }
        return ans;
    }
}

docs.title: Q2. Color Sandwich (colorSandwich)
docs.text:
Topic: strings, parsing

Sam is a cook, and he has colored breads and stuffing with which he had made some sandwiches. A sandwich can be made by keeping multiple or no stuffing between two same-colored breads like: qabcq (where abc represents stuffing and q represents coloured bread). The sandwiches are placed one over the other represented by a string S where each character depicts either bread or the stuffing. Your task is to find and return a string value representing the colour of the breads used in all sandwiches.

docs.title: Input Specification
docs.text: • input1: A string S representing the sandwiches.

docs.title: Output Specification
docs.text: • Return a string value representing the colour of the breads used in all sandwiches.

docs.title: Example 1
docs.text:
• Input: qczcquu
  • Output: qu
  • Explanation:
  • The first sandwich is qczcq: where bread is q, and the stuffing is czc.
  • The second sandwich is uu: where bread is u and no stuffing. The breads used for the sandwiches are qu. Hence qu is returned as the output.

docs.title: Example 2
docs.text:
• Input: yy
  • Output: y
  • Explanation:
  • Here, the given order of sandwiches is yy which has only one sandwich where bread is y and no stuffing. Hence y is returned as the output.

docs.title: Python 3 Solution
docs.text:
def colorSandwich(input1: str) -> str:
    ans = []
    i = 0
    n = len(input1)
    while i < n:
        bread = input1[i]
        j = input1.find(bread, i + 1)
        if j == -1:
            break
        ans.append(bread)
        i = j + 1
    return "".join(ans)

# Alternative: If unique bread colors are required, use:
# def colorSandwichUnique(input1: str) -> str:
#     ans = []
#     seen = set()
#     i = 0
#     n = len(input1)
#     while i < n:
#         bread = input1[i]
#         j = input1.find(bread, i + 1)
#         if j == -1:
#             break
#         if bread not in seen:
#             ans.append(bread)
#             seen.add(bread)
#         i = j + 1
#     return "".join(ans)

docs.title: Java Solution
docs.text:
public class UserMainCode {
    public String colorSandwich(String input1) {
        StringBuilder ans = new StringBuilder();
        int i = 0;
        int n = input1.length();
        while (i < n) {
            char bread = input1.charAt(i);
            int j = input1.indexOf(bread, i + 1);
            if (j == -1) {
                break;
            }
            ans.append(bread);
            i = j + 1;
        }
        return ans.toString();
    }
}

docs.title: Q1. Bob's Payment
docs.text:
Topic: math, greedy, implementation

Bob needs to pay a certain amount of money, denoted by an integer \(N\). However, Bob dislikes certain digits. He wants to find the minimum amount of money \(X\) (\(X \ge N\)) that he needs to pay such that \(X\) does not contain any of the digits Bob dislikes.

docs.title: Input Format:
docs.text:
• The first line contains two integers \(N\) and \(k\) denoting the amount that Bob needs to pay and the number of digits that Bob dislikes, respectively.
  • The next line contains \(k\) space-separated integers denoting the digits that Bob dislikes.

docs.title: Output Format:
docs.text: • Print a single integer denoting the minimum amount of money that Bob needs to pay.

docs.title: Sample Input:
docs.text:
100 2
0 1

docs.title: Sample Output:
docs.text: 222

docs.title: Explanation:
docs.text:
Given \(N = 100\), \(k = 2\), and dislike_digits = [0, 1]. Here Bob has to pay an amount greater than or equal to 100 by not using the digits [0, 1] that he dislikes. 222 is the minimum number that can be formed that doesn't contain [0, 1].

import sys

def main():
    # Read all inputs from standard input
    input_data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    if not input_data:
        return
    N = int(input_data[0])
    k = int(input_data[1])
    dislikes = [int(x) for x in input_data[2:2+k]]

    # Store disliked digits as strings for fast checking
    dislike_set = set(str(d) for d in dislikes)

    # Find the smallest number >= N with no disliked digits
    curr = N
    while True:
        if not any(char in dislike_set for char in str(curr)):
            print(curr)
            return
        curr += 1

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

docs.title: Q2. Double XOR Pairs
docs.text:
Topic: bit-manipulation, math

Given a positive integer \(N\). Find all pairs of integers \((x, y)\) such that: - \(1 \le x, y \le N\) - \(x + y = N\) - \(x + y = 2 \cdot (x \oplus y)\), where \(\oplus\) denotes the bitwise XOR operator.

Print all valid pairs \((x, y)\) in increasing order of \(x\). If there are no such pairs, print a single line containing -1 -1.

docs.title: Input Format:
docs.text:
• The first line contains \(T\), which represents the number of test cases.
  • For each testcase, the first line consists of a single integer \(N\) denoting the value of \(N\).

docs.title: Output Format:
docs.text: • For each testcase, print all valid pairs \((x, y)\) on a new line in increasing order of \(x\). If there are no such pairs, print a single line containing -1 -1.

docs.title: Sample Input:
docs.text:
3
4
7
144

docs.title: Explanation:
docs.text:
• For \(N = 4\): \(x + y = 4\), \(x \oplus y = 2\). The only valid pairs are \((1, 3)\) and \((3, 1)\) since: \(1 + 3 = 4\) and \(2 \cdot (1 \oplus 3) = 2 \cdot 2 = 4\). \(3 + 1 = 4\) and \(2 \cdot (3 \oplus 1) = 2 \cdot 2 = 4\). Thus, we print: 1 3 3 1

  • For \(N = 7\): No valid pairs satisfy the conditions, so we print -1 -1.

  • For \(N = 144\): There are 4 pairs satisfying the conditions: \((36, 108), (44, 100), (100, 44), (108, 36)\).

import sys

def solve():
    input_data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    if not input_data:
        return

    T = int(input_data[0])
    out = []

    for t in range(1, T + 1):
        N = int(input_data[t])

        # Check divisibility by 4.
        # Since x + y = 2*(x ^ y) and x + y = (x ^ y) + 2*(x & y)
        # we have x ^ y = 2*(x & y), so N = 4*(x & y).
        # Thus, N must be a multiple of 4.
        if N % 4 != 0:
            out.append("-1 -1")
            continue

        A = N // 4
        two_A = N // 2

        # If A and two_A share set bits, no solution exists.
        if (A & two_A) != 0:
            out.append("-1 -1")
            continue

        # Extract set bits of two_A (which is the mask for x ^ y)
        bits = []
        for i in range(30):
            if (two_A & (1 << i)):
                bits.append(1 << i)

        # Generate all submasks of two_A in strictly increasing order
        submasks = [0]
        for b in bits:
            submasks.extend([s + b for s in submasks])

        # For each submask S, construct the pair (x, y)
        for S in submasks:
            x = A + S
            y = A + (two_A - S)
            out.append(f"{x} {y}")

    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out) + "\n")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    solve()

docs.title: Q1. Dealing with Unrealistic Customer Expectations
docs.text:
Topic: client-management, expectation-setting

Your team realized that unrealistic customer expectations were being set in the sales process, leading to over-servicing. Over-servicing is affecting your organization's growth due to time spent doing unpaid work. Which of the following would you choose in this situation?

Options: - A) Roll out an incentive program for the team to stop overservicing. - B) Start charging all existing customers for all the over-service, thereby increasing revenues. - C) Implement an overage policy to set service limits and provide a framework for exception cases. - D) Deny overservicing to any customer going forward with immediate effect.

Correct Answer: C) Implement an overage policy to set service limits and provide a framework for exception cases. Explanation: Implementing a structured overage policy sets professional boundaries, defines service limits clearly, and provides a systematic way to handle exceptions. This protects the company from unpaid work while maintaining customer trust, rather than abruptly denying service or penalizing the team.

docs.title: Q2. Implementing Process Improvements
docs.text:
Topic: change-management, team-leadership

You are working as a Quality Control manager overseeing production at a large manufacturing company. Recently, a new initiative was taken up to improve production processes, and an email was sent to all the employees communicating the changes made. Some employees in the production department are still not used to the process improvements implemented. A few of them are rigid and continue to follow the earlier process. What would you do to achieve progress in implementing the improved process?

Options: - A) Roll out a communication and training plan to reach out to the employees in the production department. - B) Resend emails to all the employees, and set up posters on the production floor to reach out to everyone in the organisation. - C) Seek support from the Human Resources department to lead the change. - D) Talk to each employee one-on-one in the production department to address their challenges in adapting to the change in the process.

Correct Answer: A) Roll out a communication and training plan to reach out to the employees in the production department. Explanation: A comprehensive communication and structured training plan targets the root cause of the issue (lack of familiarity and skills). It helps the production employees understand the benefits and details of the new process, which is more effective than simply resending emails or posters.

docs.title: Q3. Prioritizing Urgent Business Calls
docs.text:
Topic: prioritization, crisis-management

You are on your way to your organisation's Strategy meet, which happens once every two years. Just before you are about to board your flight, you get two calls. One is from a long-term customer (whose office is near the airport) who has escalated the matter of your team not meeting the SLA on the latest project, which generally does not happen. The second is from your Finance team, who wants to immediately discuss pending cases of Accounts Receivables. What would your next step be?

Options: - A) Skip the Strategy meet and head to the customer site immediately, in parallel ask your Finance team to postpone the meeting to the next day so that you can be present. - B) Skip the Strategy meet and head back to the office to discuss the Receivables issue, as cash flow is critical for business; ask your account manager to visit the customer. - C) Skip the Strategy meet and head to the customer site immediately, as you always put customer needs first; ask your team to handle the Receivables discussion. - D) Proceed to the Strategy meet without changing your plans, and ask your team to handle both the issues.

Correct Answer: D) Proceed to the Strategy meet without changing your plans, and ask your team to handle both the issues. Explanation: As a leader, you must trust and delegate operational tasks to your team, especially when attending a rare and critical event like a biennial Strategy meet. Both issues (an SLA violation and Accounts Receivables) are operational and can be handled by the respective teams in your absence.

docs.title: Q4. Product Launch Strategy for Niche Markets
docs.text:
Topic: business-strategy, marketing

You work in a team that supports small businesses to build and strengthen their business strategy. A Tea estate owner is your new customer with a business plan to sell Tea online, operating from a store near the estate. The owner wants to build a business to last for a few generations in the family and be a distinct market player rather than making a huge profit. A market research study was held in a nearby city with fifteen flavours of tea from the owner's estate. The study found that six flavours were to the liking of most tasters. Two flavours were unique to this estate, which were hard to produce and available only in specific seasons. Which of the following would you recommend for the online business to run in profit?

Options: - A) Focus on premium packaging with only the two unique flavours as limited editions. - B) Focus on a limited edition launch for the two unique flavours and six flavours liked by most tasters. - C) Focus on premium packaging and launch with all fifteen flavours. - D) Focus on the six flavours liked by most tasters and market all of them as distinct flavours.

Correct Answer: B) Focus on a limited edition launch for the two unique flavours and six flavours liked by most tasters. Explanation: This hybrid approach balances volume and exclusivity. The six popular flavours ensure stable sales, while the two unique seasonal flavours build a distinct brand identity and prestige without overstretching production capabilities.

docs.title: Q5. Handling Manager's Influence on Surveys
docs.text:
Topic: ethics, team-management

Your organization is conducting an employee survey to identify the top three areas that the organization can improve upon. The most requested areas will be taken ahead for implementation. Your manager has shared his list openly and asked everyone in your team to submit the same so that those suggestions come out on top and are prioritised. Which course of action would you choose next?

Options: - A) Talk to the rest of the team, and make suggestions based on what everyone feels is important. - B) Ignore the survey, as most such employee surveys do not result in anything. - C) Follow your manager's direction and make the same suggestions. - D) Make suggestions based on your own views without consulting anyone.

Correct Answer: D) Make suggestions based on your own views without consulting anyone. Explanation: An employee survey is designed to capture honest, individual feedback to improve the organization. Copying the manager's list defeats the purpose of the survey and compromises its integrity. Submitting your own views independently is the ethical and correct course of action.

docs.title: Q6. Handling Non-Cooperative Business Partners
docs.text:
Topic: partner-management, conflict-resolution

Mr Paul is a regional sales partner for your organisation. He has been with your company since your CEO started the business two decades ago and knows the CEO personally. While he was a large partner in the beginning, as your organisation has grown, his contribution has become smaller. Of late, Paul has not been co-operating, which is draining your efforts to build your organization's brand. Multiple directives asking for improvement have yielded no results. You have to now decide whether or not to continue working with Mr Paul, which may impact the reputation of your organization and effort in finding a replacement. Which of the following will you choose as your immediate next step?

Options: - A) Continue working with Paul as is because he knows the CEO and any action you take may backfire on you. - B) Send a detailed directive to Paul explaining the expected behaviour from him. - C) Call for an in-person meeting with Paul, and ask the CEO to join the meeting to avoid any issues later on. - D) Discontinue working with Paul immediately after providing adequate notice.

Correct Answer: C) Call for an in-person meeting with Paul, and ask the CEO to join the meeting to avoid any issues later on. Explanation: Since Paul has a long-standing personal relationship with the CEO and his departure could impact the company's reputation, involving the CEO in a direct, face-to-face alignment meeting is the most diplomatic first step. It ensures transparency, respects the relationship, and prepares the ground for a smooth transition if partnership termination becomes necessary.

docs.title: Q7. Handling Unmet Milestones in Key Projects
docs.text:
Topic: project-management, client-relations

You have been appointed as a project manager for a client facing project. The client is one of the largest customers for your organisation and is also quite profitable. You have put in place a 45-day plan to meet the required timelines without any compromise in quality. However, at the halfway stage, many milestones are unmet due to some external circumstances. Select which action would you choose at this stage of the project.

Options: - A) Have an update meeting with the customer and let them know the revised timelines, but do not compromise on quality. - B) Continue with the existing plan but push the team to work harder and track progress closely. - C) Add additional resources, which increases your costs but ensures the project is delivered on time with the right quality. - D) Review which tasks can be deprioritised even if quality suffers a little, and remove them from the task list so that you can meet the timelines.

Correct Answer: A) Have an update meeting with the customer and let them know the revised timelines, but do not compromise on quality. Explanation: Transparency is key in client relationships, especially with key accounts. Informing the client of external delays and presenting realistic, revised timelines without compromising quality protects long-term trust and deliverable integrity.

docs.title: Q8. Resolving Interpersonal Conflict with Colleagues
docs.text:
Topic: interpersonal-skills, conflict-resolution

One of your colleagues has stopped interacting with you after an argument about project deliverables. You two were quite friendly before that day. You believe that something you said may have hurt the colleague personally, even though you did not intend it so. Which one of the following will you choose to do?

Options: - A) Ask your manager to intervene to set the relationship right. - B) Apologise to the colleague for any personal remarks and hope the colleague reciprocates. - C) Send a detailed email to the colleague explaining your position on the project deliverables, and hope that logic prevails. - D) Give it a few days for things to cool down, and make a gesture of peace such as inviting the colleague for coffee.

Correct Answer: D) Give it a few days for things to cool down, and make a gesture of peace such as inviting the colleague for coffee. Explanation: Giving the colleague some space allows emotions to cool down. A friendly, informal gesture like inviting them for coffee creates a low-pressure environment to clear up misunderstandings and rebuild the personal connection.

docs.title: Q9. Policy Changes During Active Negotiations
docs.text:
Topic: integrity, client-relations

During negotiations for a contract renewal, you commit to a customer for a 15% price reduction in exchange for growth in the business volume. As per the organisation's approval matrix, you are authorised to approve a price reduction of up to 20%. However, before you can get the contract closed, a new organisation policy is announced, which restricts price reduction to 10% for any customer. What would your approach be to the commitment you already made?

Options: - A) Reach out to the Finance team and ask them to back-date the 15% reduction in exchange for a favour. - B) Go back to the customer and inform them of the policy change, and try to negotiate for a 10% reduction only. - C) Restructure the deal by breaking the contract into two parts and showing them as a new contract which is not bound by the new policy, thereby keeping your commitment. - D) Write to senior management to get approval as an exceptional case, since you already made the commitment.

Correct Answer: D) Write to senior management to get approval as an exceptional case, since you already made the commitment. Explanation: Seeking approval from senior management as an exceptional case demonstrates both professional integrity (honoring your word to the client) and respect for company policies. Back-dating or breaking contracts are unethical workarounds, and renegotiating immediately damages the company's credibility.

docs.title: Q10. Unexpected Resignation during a Specialized Project
docs.text:
Topic: resource-management, project-continuity

Your team just started working on a specialised project after receiving the required training. One of the resources, Mr Ram, resigns suddenly with a plan to move to his hometown in 15 days to care for his unwell parents. As the project head, what would you choose to do in this situation?

Options: - A) Start looking for potential candidates immediately with a focus on readily available candidates, and hire the first decent candidate you find. - B) Distribute Ram's tasks to other team members and ask Ram to train them before leaving. - C) Insist Mr Ram cannot leave before finding a suitable replacement for himself and providing complete training. - D) Negotiate with Mr Ram to work remotely, until the HR team finds a suitable replacement.

Correct Answer: D) Negotiate with Mr Ram to work remotely, until the HR team finds a suitable replacement. Explanation: Given that the project is specialized and the team just received training, finding and training a replacement in 15 days is highly impractical. Allowing Ram to work remotely to care for his parents supports his personal needs while ensuring project continuity and knowledge preservation.

docs.title: Q11. Rebranding vs. Maintaining Brand Values
docs.text:
Topic: brand-strategy, marketing

Your organization is the market leader in an industry where the quality of products matters a lot, and you enjoy a strong brand reputation. Your smaller competitors have recently rebranded themselves and have launched an aggressive marketing campaign, in an effort to gain market share. There are growing murmurs within your company that you should rebrand to show to the market that you are also trendy. What would you recommend to your management?

Options: - A) Go ahead with the rebranding because change is what the market expects. - B) Reach out to all your existing customers and offer discounts. - C) Increase your marketing spends but continue with your brand values as they have worked in the past. - D) Do not make any changes as it may confuse the existing clients.

Correct Answer: C) Increase your marketing spends but continue with your brand values as they have worked in the past. Explanation: Rebranding a market leader with a strong reputation just because competitors are doing so is risky and unnecessary. Instead, increasing marketing spend to reaffirm and highlight your core brand values (like quality) will counter the competitors' campaigns without diluting your established brand equity.

docs.title: Q12. Managing Team Promotion & Resource Allocation
docs.text:
Topic: team-leadership, resource-management

Your manager has announced a new project may come to your team as final discussions are underway with the customer. It is a big project with high visibility, and can set you up for a promotion. Your team is already busy working on multiple projects, but you have the required technical capabilities. As a team leader, what would you want to know first when the announcement is made?

Options: - A) The size of the deal and the revenue the project will bring in for your organisation. - B) Know what support you can expect from the organisation to complete the project. - C) The duration and deliverables of the project, to be sure you can deliver. - D) Know which other teams are competing for the project, so that you can talk to your manager to improve your chances of getting the project.

Correct Answer: C) The duration and deliverables of the project, to be sure you can deliver. Explanation: As a team leader, your primary responsibility is delivery and quality. Understanding the project duration and deliverables first allows you to assess resource feasibility and plan capacity, ensuring you do not overcommit a busy team.

docs.title: Q13. Rewarding the Team & Trip Planning
docs.text:
Topic: team-engagement, operational-efficiency

Your 30-member team has exceeded the annual targets, and as a reward, the senior management has approved a foreign trip. A destination has been decided, which is new to all of you. Which one of the following would you choose for everyone to have a great travel experience?

Options: - A) Hire a professional travel agent to plan the entire trip, even if it costs more. - B) Ask one of the junior team members to take some time out to plan the trip. - C) Call the entire team for a few meetings to jointly discuss and finalise the details. - D) Request a friend, who runs a visa arrangement business, to plan the trip as a personal favour.

Correct Answer: A) Hire a professional travel agent to plan the entire trip, even if it costs more. Explanation: Planning a foreign trip for a large group of 30 people is a complex task. Hiring a professional travel agent ensures a hassle-free, well-organized experience, allowing the team to fully enjoy their reward without burdening internal team members.

docs.title: Q14. Appointing Deputy Project Manager
docs.text:
Topic: leadership, conflict-avoidance

You have been recently appointed as the manager of a new project. The general norm in the company is to have a team leader who works as a deputy to the project manager. There are only two worthy candidates who suit the requirement, however, you suspect that appointing one as the team leader will discourage the other and stop them from giving their best. What would you do under these circumstances?

Options: - A) Decide not to announce a team leader at the start of the project, and keep watching both the candidates' performance. - B) Pick one of the two who you think is a better candidate and make the decision public. - C) Ignore both candidates and pick a third candidate which will avoid the conflict. - D) Tell your preferred candidate your decision privately so as to avoid the other candidate being disappointed.

Correct Answer: A) Decide not to announce a team leader at the start of the project, and keep watching both the candidates' performance. Explanation: Delaying the appointment allows both candidates to showcase their capabilities on the live project. This keeps them motivated, promotes healthy competition, and gives you more objective performance data to make the final decision.

docs.title: Q15. Dealing with an Out-of-Character Outburst
docs.text:
Topic: empathy, team-management

One of your colleagues said something rude while addressing the team in a general conversation at work. This colleague was generally known to be a soft spoken person and had not exhibited similar behavior in the past. As a team member, what will you do next?

Options: - A) Bring it up in the next meeting so that the rest of the team does not commit a similar mistake. - B) Talk to the colleague separately to understand the reason behind the outburst, and offer any help if needed. - C) Probe if the colleague was undergoing any stressful situation which may have led to the incident. - D) Ignore it, as it was a one off and the colleague was probably just blowing off some steam.

Correct Answer: B) Talk to the colleague separately to understand the reason behind the outburst, and offer any help if needed. Explanation: Since this behavior is out of character, a private, empathetic conversation helps you understand if they are facing personal or professional stress, allowing you to offer support rather than alienating them or publicizing the slip-up.

docs.title: Q16. Responding to Senior Management Dissatisfaction
docs.text:
Topic: communication, responsibility

You receive an email from your senior management expressing their dissatisfaction with a recent project delivered by your team. Your team manager is on leave for a week. As a senior member of the team, which one of the following would you choose to do?

Options: - A) Schedule a meeting of the senior management team with the entire project team so that each team member gets an opportunity to present their viewpoint. - B) Respond immediately with an apology email. - C) Discuss with the team internally and respond with a short note highlighting the issues in the project. - D) Wait for the manager to come back from leave and respond, as you responding might make the issue bigger.

Correct Answer: C) Discuss with the team internally and respond with a short note highlighting the issues in the project. Explanation: As a senior team member, taking ownership by acknowledging the feedback, discussing it internally with the team to identify gaps, and sending a concise response showing action is the most professional path. Waiting a week or sending an empty apology shows lack of proactiveness.

docs.title: Q17. Assisting Customers Outside Assigned Role
docs.text:
Topic: customer-first, proactiveness

Your company is celebrating "Foundation Day" and has invited a few customers to your office. Some employees have been given the opportunity to take care of customers on that day. You too wanted that opportunity but got missed out. On "Foundation Day", you reach the office early and notice one of the customers (who was also early) was wandering about the lobby area, unsure of what to do. One of your colleagues was supposed to take care of this customer. Which of the following do you choose to do?

Options: - A) Introduce yourself to the customer and keep them company until your colleague arrives. - B) Proceed as usual to your work, as you are not aware of the special arrangements made for customers. - C) Inform security to escort the customer to a meeting room and ask them to wait until your colleague arrives. - D) Call your colleague to update that their customer had reached, and ask the colleague to reach the office immediately.

Correct Answer: A) Introduce yourself to the customer and keep them company until your colleague arrives. Explanation: Putting the customer first and ensuring they feel welcomed reflects positively on the organization. Keeping them company is proactive, hospitable, and handles the situation professionally until the designated colleague arrives.

docs.title: Q18. Last-Minute Complex Change Requests
docs.text:
Topic: client-relations, project-integrity

You work for a technology company which builds websites for customers. On one particular project, you received a last-minute change request from the client just one day before the launch of their website. The client expects to launch the site with the change implemented. The change however is complex and can be released only after intense testing, which is not possible with your current team. Which of the following do you choose to do?

Options: - A) Deny the client's request, as it is risky to make changes at the last minute without delaying the launch. - B) Implement the change before the launch date without doing the required testing. - C) Initiate the change implementation and let the client know the website launch is delayed. - D) Implement the change by pulling in more manpower to work on the testing, even though it is not approved in your organisation's budget.

Correct Answer: C) Initiate the change implementation and let the client know the website launch is delayed. Explanation: Implementing untested complex changes on launch day is extremely risky and can lead to site crashes. The best path is to agree to the change but communicate clearly that a delay is required to ensure proper quality control and testing.

docs.title: Q19. Challenging a Process Change
docs.text:
Topic: assertiveness, professional-integrity

After a senior management discussion with the client, your manager asks you to implement a new step in the process of an ongoing project. You are quite sure that this step would interfere with the quality and timeline of completing the project. Which one of the following would you choose to do?

Options: - A) Agree to implement the new step after a couple of months, but use that time to gather evidence to prove the new step would impact quality. - B) Request for a discussion to explain the possible problems with the new step before doing any implementation. - C) Refuse to implement the new step as it would cause delays. - D) Implement the new step as it is a direct ask from both the client and senior management.

Correct Answer: B) Request for a discussion to explain the possible problems with the new step before doing any implementation. Explanation: Discussing your concerns proactively and explaining the potential negative impact on quality and timeline is the most constructive response. It allows you to align with your manager before any damage is done, rather than blindly implementing or flatly refusing.

docs.title: Q20. Declining Productivity in a Colleague
docs.text:
Topic: empathy, teamwork

You have noticed that the productivity of one of your colleagues has recently been dropping off. You get to know that the colleague is going through a stressful time because of a parent's recent diagnosis of a serious medical problem. What would be your next course of action?

Options: - A) Probe the colleague regarding the nature of the illness and provide references of some experts you may know. - B) Talk to the colleague and explain that personal matters should be kept separate from professional matters. - C) Request your manager to replace the colleague in question until the personal situation improves. - D) Share an upsetting health situation from your own experience and explain how you effectively overcame it.

Correct Answer: A) Probe the colleague regarding the nature of the illness and provide references of some experts you may know. Explanation: Demonstrating empathy and offering practical help (like expert medical contacts) shows genuine support for a colleague during a difficult time, fostering team bond and assisting them in resolving their personal stress.

docs.title: Q21. Angle of Elevation & Speed Ratio
docs.text:
Topic: trigonometry, relative-speed

There are two towers AB and CD (A and C being their tops and B and D being their feet). The angle of elevation of C from B is 30° and the angle of elevation of A from D is 60°. An eagle and a kite start flying at the same moment from the tops of towers AB and CD respectively towards the foot of the other tower along a straight line and at uniform speeds. If they meet at one point along their flight, find the ratio of their speeds.

Options: - A) \(\sqrt{3} : 1\) - B) \(3\sqrt{3} : 1\) - C) \(3\sqrt{3} : 2\) - D) \(3 : 1\)

Correct Answer: B) \(3\sqrt{3} : 1\)

Solution: Let the distance between the feet of the towers, \(BD = x\). 1. Height of Tower CD: $\(\tan 30^\circ = \frac{CD}{x} \implies CD = \frac{x}{\sqrt{3}}\)$ 2. Height of Tower AB: $\(\tan 60^\circ = \frac{AB}{x} \implies AB = x\sqrt{3}\)$

Let B be at origin \((0, 0)\) and D be at \((x, 0)\) in Cartesian coordinates. * Top of tower AB: \(A(0, x\sqrt{3})\) * Top of tower CD: \(C(x, \frac{x}{\sqrt{3}})\) * Path of the eagle: Line segment AD connecting \(A(0, x\sqrt{3})\) and \(D(x, 0)\). $\(\text{Equation of AD: } y = -\sqrt{3}x_{coord} + x\sqrt{3}\)$ * Path of the kite: Line segment CB connecting \(C(x, \frac{x}{\sqrt{3}})\) and \(B(0, 0)\). $\(\text{Equation of CB: } y = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}x_{coord}\)$

They meet at the intersection point \(P(x_p, y_p)\): $\(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} x_p = -\sqrt{3}x_p + x\sqrt{3} \implies x_p = -3x_p + 3x \implies 4x_p = 3x \implies x_p = \frac{3}{4}x\)$ $\(y_p = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}x\)$

  1. Distances Flown:
  2. Eagle distance (\(d_1\) from \(A\) to \(P\)): $\(d_1 = \sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{4}x\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}x - x\sqrt{3}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\frac{9}{16}x^2 + \frac{27}{16}x^2} = \sqrt{\frac{36}{16}x^2} = \frac{3}{2}x\)$
  3. Kite distance (\(d_2\) from \(C\) to \(P\)): $\(d_2 = \sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{4}x - x\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}x - \frac{x}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{16}x^2 + \frac{3}{144}x^2} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{12}x^2} = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{3}}\)$

Since they started at the same time and met, their speeds \(v_1\) and \(v_2\) are proportional to the distances: $\(\frac{v_1}{v_2} = \frac{d_1}{d_2} = \frac{\frac{3}{2}x}{\frac{x}{2\sqrt{3}}} = \frac{3}{2} \times 2\sqrt{3} = 3\sqrt{3} : 1\)$

docs.title: Q22. Average Weight Replacement
docs.text:
Topic: averages

The average weight of 10 students is increased by half a kg when one of the students weighing 50 kg is replaced by a new student. Find the weight of the new student.

Options: - A) 55 kg - B) 45 kg - C) 60 kg - D) 40 kg

Correct Answer: A) 55 kg

Solution: Let the original sum of weights of 10 students be \(S\). $\(\text{New Sum} = S - 50 + W_{new}\)$ Since average increases by \(0.5\) kg: $\(\frac{S - 50 + W_{new}}{10} = \frac{S}{10} + 0.5 \implies S - 50 + W_{new} = S + 5 \implies W_{new} = 55 \text{ kg}\)$

docs.title: Q23. Compounded Installments
docs.text:
Topic: compound-interest, installments

An LCD is available for Rs 25000 cash payment or for Rs 3500 cash down payment and three equal annual installments. If the shopkeeper charges interest at the rate of 12% per annum compounded annually, calculate the approximate amount of each installment.

Options: - A) Rs 8895 - B) Rs 8952 - C) Rs 8859 - D) Rs 8592

Correct Answer: B) Rs 8952

Solution: * Principal outstanding \(P = 25000 - 3500 = 21500\). * Rate of interest \(r = 12\% = 0.12\). Let the annual installment be \(x\). $\(P = \frac{x}{1+r} + \frac{x}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{x}{(1+r)^3}\)$ $\(21500 = x \left[ \frac{1}{1.12} + \frac{1}{1.12^2} + \frac{1}{1.12^3} \right]\)$ $\(21500 = x [0.89286 + 0.79719 + 0.71178] = 2.40183 x\)$ $\(x = \frac{21500}{2.40183} \approx 8951.5 \approx \text{Rs } 8952\)$

docs.title: Q24. Profit and Cost under Seasonal Sale
docs.text:
Topic: profit-and-loss

A watch dealer incurs an expense of Rs. 150 for producing every watch. He also incurs an additional expenditure of Rs. 30,000, which is independent of the number of watches produced. If he is able to sell a watch during the season, he sells it for Rs. 250. If he fails to do so, he has to sell each watch for Rs. 100. If he is able to sell only 1200 out of the 1500 watches he has made in the season, then in the season he has made a profit of:

Options: - A) Rs. 75,000 - B) Rs. 45,000 - C) Rs. 90,000 - D) Rs. 60,000

Correct Answer: A) Rs. 75,000

Solution: 1. Total Cost (TC): $\(TC = 1500 \times 150 + 30000 = 225000 + 30000 = \text{Rs. } 255,000\)$ 2. Total Revenue (TR): * 1200 watches sold during the season at Rs. 250 each. * \(1500 - 1200 = 300\) watches sold out of season at Rs. 100 each. $\(TR = 1200 \times 250 + 300 \times 100 = 300000 + 30000 = \text{Rs. } 330,000\)$ 3. Profit: $\(\text{Profit} = TR - TC = 330000 - 255000 = \text{Rs. } 75,000\)$

docs.title: Q25. Basic Percentages
docs.text:
Topic: percentage

If 25% of 200 + 30% of x = 80, then what is the value of x?

Options: - A) 120 - B) 125 - C) 50 - D) 100

Correct Answer: D) 100

Solution: $\(0.25 \times 200 + 0.30 \times x = 80\)$ $\(50 + 0.30 x = 80 \implies 0.30 x = 30 \implies x = 100\)$

docs.title: Q26. Direct Variation and Rainfall
docs.text:
Topic: variation, proportions

The drop-in temperature in degrees Celsius from the previous day varies directly as the square root of the rainfall in mm. On April 22nd, 25 mm of rainfall was recorded. That day the temperature recorded was 32°C. It was quite a relief for the citizens who witnessed 42°C the day before. On May 16th, there was a rainfall of 36 mm. The temperature on May 15th was 44°C. What was the temperature on May 16th? (in °C)

Options: - A) 16 - B) 18 - C) 32 - D) 20

Correct Answer: C) 32

Solution: Let drop-in temperature be \(D\) and rainfall be \(R\). $\(D = k\sqrt{R} \text{ (where } k \text{ is constant)}\)$ On April 22nd: $\(D = 42 - 32 = 10^\circ\text{C}, \quad R = 25 \text{ mm}\)$ $\(10 = k\sqrt{25} \implies 5k = 10 \implies k = 2\)$ On May 16th: $\(R = 36 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Previous temp } (T_{15}) = 44^\circ\text{C}\)$ $\(D = 2\sqrt{36} = 2 \times 6 = 12^\circ\text{C}\)$ $\(\text{Temperature on May 16th} = 44 - 12 = 32^\circ\text{C}\)$

docs.title: Q27. Wheel Revolutions
docs.text:
Topic: circles, ratios

A wheel has a diameter of x inches and a second wheel has a diameter of y inches. The first wheel covers a distance of 'd' feet in 100 revolutions. How many revolutions does the second wheel make in covering 'd' feet?

Options: - A) \(100x - y\) - B) \(100x / y\) - C) \(100y / x\) - D) \(100xy\)

Correct Answer: B) \(100x / y\)

Solution: Distance covered by wheel 1: $\(d = 100 \times \pi x\)$ Distance covered by wheel 2 in \(N_2\) revolutions: $\(d = N_2 \times \pi y\)$ Equating the two: $\(100 \pi x = N_2 \pi y \implies N_2 = \frac{100x}{y}\)$

docs.title: Q28. Detour & Speed Ratios
docs.text:
Topic: time-speed-distance

Raj's home is 10 km from his office. He started walking at a uniform speed to reach his office exactly on time, planning to walk for 2 hours. After walking d km, he realized he forgot an important file at home; he turned around, walked back the same d km, picked up the file, and then cycled to his office at twice his walking speed. Despite this detour, he reached his office on time. d is what percentage of the total distance between his home and office?

Options: - A) 20% - B) 32% - C) 36% - D) 25%

Correct Answer: D) 25%

Solution: * Planned walking speed \(v = \frac{10 \text{ km}}{2 \text{ hours}} = 5\) km/h. * Actual trip breakdown: 1. Walks \(d\) km at 5 km/h: time = \(\frac{d}{5}\) 2. Walks back \(d\) km at 5 km/h: time = \(\frac{d}{5}\) 3. Cycles 10 km (from home to office) at \(2v = 10\) km/h: time = \(\frac{10}{10} = 1\) hour. * Total actual time = Planned time: $\(\frac{d}{5} + \frac{d}{5} + 1 = 2 \implies \frac{2d}{5} = 1 \implies d = 2.5 \text{ km}\)$ * Percentage of total distance: $\(\frac{d}{\text{Total Distance}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.5}{10} \times 100\% = 25\%\)$

docs.title: Q29. Sum of Average Heart Rate
docs.text:
Topic: data-interpretation, bar-graph

Refer to the bar graph representing daily heart rates of 4 patients: Ravi, Priya, Arjun, Neetu. * Day 3 Data: Ravi = 70, Priya = 80, Arjun = 74, Neetu = 79.

What is the sum of average heart rate of all the four patients on Day 3?

Options: - A) 310 bpm - B) 324 bpm - C) 288 bpm - D) 303 bpm

Correct Answer: D) 303 bpm

Solution: $\(\text{Sum} = 70 + 80 + 74 + 79 = 303 \text{ bpm}\)$

docs.title: Q30. Heart Rate Ratios
docs.text:
Topic: data-interpretation, ratios

What is the ratio of Arjun's average heart rate on Day 2 to his average heart rate on Day 3? * Arjun's Data: Day 2 = 77 bpm, Day 3 = 74 bpm.

Options: - A) 77:74 - B) 75:79 - C) 36:37 - D) 37:36

Correct Answer: A) 77:74

docs.title: Q31. Percentage Increase of Heart Rate
docs.text:
Topic: data-interpretation, percentages

By what percentage did Ravi's average heart rate increase from Day 1 to Day 2? * Ravi's Data: Day 1 = 72 bpm, Day 2 = 75 bpm.

Options: - A) 1.39% - B) 5.56% - C) 2.78% - D) 4.17%

Correct Answer: D) 4.17%

Solution: $\(\text{Increase} = 75 - 72 = 3 \text{ bpm}\)$ $\(\text{Percentage Increase} = \frac{3}{72} \times 100\% = 4.167\% \approx 4.17\%\)$

docs.title: Q32. Profit Maximization on Line Graph
docs.text:
Topic: data-interpretation, line-graph

Refer to the line graph of toys manufactured: A=324, B=200, C=460, D=250, E=500.

If company A sells each toy for Rs. 45, then the company will face a loss of Rs. 1620. At what price would they sell each toy so that company A would get Rs. 4860 as a profit?

Options: - A) Rs. 30 - B) Rs. 65 - C) Rs. 50 - D) Rs. 45

Correct Answer: B) Rs. 65

Solution: * Toys manufactured by A = 324. * Revenue at Rs. 45 = \(324 \times 45 = \text{Rs. } 14,580\). * Since there is a loss of Rs. 1620, Total Cost (TC) = \(14,580 + 1620 = \text{Rs. } 16,200\). * Cost Price per toy = \(\frac{16200}{324} = \text{Rs. } 50\). * Desired profit = Rs. 4860. * Desired Revenue = \(16200 + 4860 = \text{Rs. } 21,060\). * Selling Price (SP) required = \(\frac{21060}{324} = \text{Rs. } 65\).

docs.title: Q33. Toys Sold at Discount and Original Price
docs.text:
Topic: data-interpretation, percentages

In company C, some of the toys are sold at their original price and some of them are sold at Rs. 120, which is a price after giving a 20% discount on the original price. If the amount received by company C after selling all the toys is Rs. 63600, then how many toys are sold at the original price? All the toys manufactured in company C are sold. * Company C Toys: 460.

Options: - A) 320 - B) 240 - C) 280 - D) 490

Correct Answer: C) 280

Solution: * Let the original price be \(P\). $\(P \times (1 - 0.20) = 120 \implies 0.8P = 120 \implies P = \text{Rs. } 150\)$ * Let the number of toys sold at original price be \(x\). $\(150x + 120(460 - x) = 63600\)$ $\(30x + 55200 = 63600 \implies 30x = 8400 \implies x = 280\)$

docs.title: Q34. Ratios of Percentages Sold
docs.text:
Topic: data-interpretation, ratios

The number of toys sold in company B is equal to the number of toys left unsold in company E. If 50% of the toys in B are sold, then what is the ratio between the percentage of toys sold in E and the percentage of toys unsold in B? * Toys manufactured: B=200, E=500.

Options: - A) 7:9 - B) 4:7 - C) 5:3 - D) 8:5

Correct Answer: D) 8:5

Solution: 1. Company B: * Toys sold = \(50\% \text{ of } 200 = 100\). * Toys unsold = 100. * Percentage of toys unsold in B = \(50\%\). 2. Company E: * Toys unsold = Toys sold in B = 100. * Toys sold = \(500 - 100 = 400\). * Percentage of toys sold in E = \(\frac{400}{500} \times 100\% = 80\%\). 3. Ratio: $\(\text{Ratio} = \frac{80\%}{50\%} = 8:5\)$

docs.title: Q35. Profit under Discount Scheme
docs.text:
Topic: data-interpretation, profit-and-loss

When a 40% discount is given on each toy (each toy has a list price of Rs. 150), company D earns a 20% profit. If he sold each toy at a price that was 20% above the cost, then what would be the profit earned by company D? All the toys manufactured by company D were sold. * Company D Toys: 250.

Options: - A) Rs. 2875 - B) Rs. 2500 - C) Rs. 3750 - D) Rs. 4520

Correct Answer: C) Rs. 3750

Solution: * List Price = Rs. 150. * Selling Price (after 40% discount) = \(150 \times 0.6 = \text{Rs. } 90\). * This gives a 20% profit, so Cost Price (CP) per toy = \(\frac{90}{1.2} = \text{Rs. } 75\). * New selling scheme: SP is 20% above cost. $\(\text{New Profit per toy} = 20\% \text{ of } 75 = \text{Rs. } 15\)$ $\(\text{Total Profit} = 250 \text{ toys} \times 15 = \text{Rs. } 3750\)$

docs.title: Q36. Sentence Correction: Convince
docs.text:
Topic: subject-verb-agreement

Identify if the underlined part in the below sentence is incorrect. If yes, choose the option which makes the sentence grammatically correct. If no, choose the option that is the same as the underlined part.

After trying to convince him for a long time, I realized that he was one of those people who never listens to reason.

Options: - A) who never listen to reason - B) who never listens to a reason - C) who never listen to reasoning - D) who never listens to reason

Correct Answer: A) who never listen to reason Explanation: The relative pronoun "who" refers back to the plural antecedent "people". Therefore, the verb that follows must also be plural ("listen" instead of "listens").

docs.title: Q37. Error Detection: Guitarists
docs.text:
Topic: tense-agreement

Choose the phrase which is wrong or inappropriate in the context of the sentence:

Few guitarists can (A) claim to have developed a style (B) as distinctive as his, and rarely has such a weak (C) voice teaching is used (D) so evocatively.

Options: - A) D - B) B - C) A - D) C

Correct Answer: A) D Explanation: The helper verb "has" requires the past participle "been used" instead of the present passive construction "is used" (e.g., "rarely has such a weak voice been used...").

docs.title: Q38. Error Detection: Blind Man
docs.text:
Topic: verbs-of-perception

Choose the phrase which is wrong or inappropriate in the context of the sentence:

I saw (A) the blind man cross (B) the busy road near the (C) bazaar without any help. (D)

Options: - A) D - B) B - C) C - D) A

Correct Answer: B) B (Note: If the test had "crossed" in part B, it would be the error. If it is "cross", the sentence is grammatically correct.) Explanation: Verbs of perception like "saw" take a bare infinitive ("cross") or present participle ("crossing") rather than a past tense verb ("crossed"). As written in the graphic with "cross", there is no error. If it was "crossed" in the original question, "crossed" is incorrect.

docs.title: Q39. Error Detection: Social Media
docs.text:
Topic: subject-verb-agreement

Choose the phrase which is wrong or inappropriate in the context of the sentence:

People are (A) greatly influenced by (B) the content they sees (C) on (D) social media.

Options: - A) D - B) B - C) C - D) A

Correct Answer: C) C Explanation: The pronoun "they" is plural, so it must be paired with the plural verb "see" instead of the singular "sees".

docs.title: Q40. Sentence Arrangement: Indignant Neighbours
docs.text:
Topic: para-jumbles

Choose the most logical order of the given sentences to construct a coherent paragraph:

A. The fact that he could find absolutely nothing to substantiate their wild claims made no difference. B. We always gave the poor man a cup of tea, and he grew quite fond of some of the animals. C. The neighbours, now thoroughly indignant, kept bombarding the local health authorities. D. On an average, twice a week, the poor inspector was forced to come up to the house.

Options: - A) CDAB - B) CADB - C) ADBC - D) DBAC

Correct Answer: A) CDAB Explanation: Sentence C sets the context (neighbours complain to health authorities). D logically follows, stating that as a result, the inspector visited. A continues by mentioning what the inspector found (nothing), and B concludes with the hosts' relationship with the inspector (giving him tea).

docs.title: Q41. Sentence Arrangement: Giant Calendar
docs.text:
Topic: para-jumbles

Choose the most logical order of the given sentences to construct a coherent paragraph:

A. In the narrow space between the dark silhouettes of two buildings, as in the crack of a door, he saw the page of a gigantic calendar suspended in the sky. B. The purpose was that citizens might tell the day of the month as they told the hours of the day, by glancing up at a public tower. C. In the rusty light of this evening's sunset, the rectangle said: September B. D. A white rectangle, thus, hung over the city, imparting the date to the men in the streets below. E. It was the calendar that the mayor of New York had erected last year on the top of a building.

Options: - A) AEBDC - B) AEBCD - C) AECDB - D) AECBD

Correct Answer: A) AEBDC Explanation: A introduces the gigantic calendar. E specifies which calendar it was. B states the purpose of the calendar. D introduces "a white rectangle" representing the calendar page, and C refers to "the rectangle" showing the date. Since D introduces "a white rectangle" (indefinite article) and C refers to it as "the rectangle" (definite article), D must precede C.

docs.title: Q42. Preposition Fill in the Blank: Seated
docs.text:
Topic: prepositions

Fill in the blank below with the most appropriate word:

She was seated ____ two old women on the train to Kalimpong.

Correct Answer: between Explanation: The preposition "between" is used when referring to two distinct entities (the "two old women").

docs.title: Q43. Sentence Arrangement: School Uniform
docs.text:
Topic: para-jumbles

Choose the most logical order of the remaining words/phrases to construct a coherent sentence. (The position of 1 is fixed).

  1. A school P. pride in their appearance Q. students to R. and take S. dress smartly T. uniform teaches

Options: - A) TQSRP - B) PQRST - C) RSPQT - D) QRSPT

Correct Answer: A) TQSRP Explanation: Starting with "1. A school": T ("uniform teaches") -> Q ("students to") -> S ("dress smartly") -> R ("and take") -> P ("pride in their appearance"). The complete sentence is: "A school uniform teaches students to dress smartly and take pride in their appearance."

docs.title: Q44. Reading Comprehension: Jungian Therapy Goal
docs.text:
Topic: reading-comprehension

Refer to the Jungian therapy passage.

Jungian therapy aims at:

Options: - A) helping one become a mentally sound individual. - B) forming a pattern of a person's dreams. - C) assimilating a person's innate cultural knowledge. - D) eradicating all bad past experiences from a person's memory.

Correct Answer: A) helping one become a mentally sound individual. Explanation: According to the passage, the goal of Jungian therapy is "individuation," which integrates conscious and unconscious parts of the mind to live a healthy, stable, and meaningful life.

docs.title: Q45. Reading Comprehension: Collective Unconscious
docs.text:
Topic: reading-comprehension

According to Jung, the collective unconscious:

Options: - A) is related to a person's past life. - B) is responsible for all man's actions. - C) is the most impressionable. - D) is a common human feature.

Correct Answer: D) is a common human feature. Explanation: The text states that the collective unconscious is "a collective unconscious all people share" and contains "human universals that we are all born with," making it a common human feature.

docs.title: Q46. Reading Comprehension: Word Association Tests
docs.text:
Topic: reading-comprehension

Word association tests would help a psychologist:

Options: - A) evaluate the subconscious associations made by the person. - B) ascertain the person's subconscious desires. - C) evaluate the conscious choice of words made by the person. - D) judge the person's passive vocabulary.

Correct Answer: A) evaluate the subconscious associations made by the person. Explanation: The passage indicates that therapists use word association tests to "better understand their client's unconscious mind," which involves evaluating the subconscious connections they make.

docs.title: Q47. Preposition Fill in the Blank: Good at
docs.text:
Topic: prepositions

Fill in the blank below with the most appropriate word:

She has always been very good ____ Maths and plans to pursue it further.

Correct Answer: at Explanation: The adjective "good" is idiomatically followed by "at" when referring to a skill or subject (e.g., "good at Maths").

docs.title: Q48. Conjunction Fill in the Blank: List
docs.text:
Topic: conjunctions

Fill in the blank below with the most appropriate word:

Hema made omelets, bread ____ coffee for breakfast yesterday.

Correct Answer: and Explanation: The coordinating conjunction "and" is used to connect the final item in a list.

docs.title: Q49. Sentence Correction: Cheerfulness
docs.text:
Topic: phrasal-verbs

Identify if the underlined part in the below sentence is incorrect.

I never asked from these more cheerfulness, just a short break from the moaning and groaning.

Options: - A) from these more - B) for this much - C) from this much - D) for these much

Correct Answer: B) for this much Explanation: The verb "ask" takes the preposition "for" to mean requesting something. Since "cheerfulness" is uncountable, "much" is the correct quantifier.

docs.title: Q50. Sentence Correction: Percentages & Plurals
docs.text:
Topic: adjectives

Identify if the underlined part in the below sentence is incorrect.

Few than 40% students made it to the next semester.

Options: - A) Lesser than 40% of the - B) Less than 40% of the - C) Fewer than 40% of - D) Few than 40%

Correct Answer: B) Less than 40% of the Explanation: "Few" cannot be comparative; it must be "fewer" or "less". When expressing a percentage of a group, "Less than 40% of the" is correct and standard.

docs.title: Q51. Machine Input-Output
docs.text:
Topic: input-output

A word and number arrangement machine when given an input line of words and numbers rearranges them following a particular rule in each step:

Input: poland haiti 32 jermany 95 50 belgium 65 Step (1): belgium poland haiti 32 jermany 95 50 65 Step (2): belgium 95 poland haiti 32 jermany 50 65 Step (3): belgium 95 haiti poland 32 jermany 50 65 Step (4): belgium 95 haiti 65 poland 32 jermany 50 Step (5): belgium 95 haiti 65 jermany poland 32 50 Step (6): belgium 95 haiti 65 jermany 50 poland 32

Question: How many steps will be required to complete the rearrangement of the following input? Input: Denmark 54 Latvia 27 Peru 64 82 Austrilla

Options: - A) 5 - B) 4 - C) 6 - D) 7

Correct Answer: A) 5

Solution: * Rule: Alphabetically smallest word is placed at the front (odd steps), and largest number is placed next (even steps). If the target element is already in position, we proceed to the next element without consuming an extra step. * Target Word Order: Austrilla, Denmark, Latvia, Peru * Target Number Order: 82, 64, 54, 27 * Trace: * Start: Denmark 54 Latvia 27 Peru 64 82 Austrilla * Step 1: Austrilla Denmark 54 Latvia 27 Peru 64 82 * Step 2: Austrilla 82 Denmark 54 Latvia 27 Peru 64 * Step 3: (Denmark is already in place. Next is number 64): Austrilla 82 Denmark 64 54 Latvia 27 Peru * Step 4: (Next is word Latvia): Austrilla 82 Denmark 64 Latvia 54 27 Peru * Step 5: (Next is number 54, already in place. Next is word Peru): Austrilla 82 Denmark 64 Latvia 54 Peru 27 * (The remaining number 27 is already at the end. Rearrangement is complete in 5 steps.)

docs.title: Q52. World Destinations: Turkey
docs.text:
Topic: grid-puzzle

Five friends (Anita, Charles, Lucas, Jim, Brian) went to 4 world destinations (US, Turkey, Australia, New Zealand) to enjoy different adventure sports (Hang Gliding, Storm Chasing, Surfing, ParaSailing, Bungee jumping). One destination has two friends. * (I) Anita did not go to the US or Turkey, also she did not do Surfing. The one who did Hang Gliding is not Charles. * (II) The two friends who were at the same destination tried two different sports, Storm Chasing and Hang Gliding. * (III) Charles was in Australia and had no companion. * (IV) Lucas tried ParaSailing. Jim was not in Turkey or New Zealand. * (V) The one who did Bungee jumping in New Zealand is not Brian. * (VI) Brian did not go to Turkey.

Question: Which of the friends given in the options was in Turkey?

Options: - A) Lucas - B) Charles - C) Anita - D) Jim

Correct Answer: A) Lucas

Solution: * Charles is in Australia (alone). * Anita cannot be in US, Turkey, or Australia (no companion). Thus, Anita is in New Zealand. * Jim cannot be in Turkey, New Zealand, or Australia. Thus, Jim is in US. * Brian cannot be in Turkey or Australia. * If Brian is in New Zealand, New Zealand is the double destination (Anita and Brian). But the double destination must have Storm Chasing and Hang Gliding. Since Anita did Bungee jumping (below), this is impossible. * Thus, Brian is in US. * This leaves Lucas, who must be in Turkey. * Sports assignment check: * Lucas: ParaSailing (Turkey) * Jim and Brian: Storm Chasing and Hang Gliding (US) * Anita: Bungee jumping (New Zealand) * Charles: Surfing (Australia) All clues are fully satisfied. Lucas was in Turkey.

docs.title: Q55. Consonant Reordering
docs.text:
Topic: word-play

The word 'PROLIFERATION' is re-written by reversing the last seven letters and the remaining letters in the alphabetical order. How many consonants will be there after removing the letters which are in even numbered places?

Options: - A) Two - B) Four - C) Three - D) Five

Correct Answer: B) Four

Solution: * Original word: P R O L I F E R A T I O N (13 letters) * First 6 letters: P R O L I F \(\rightarrow\) alphabetical: F I L O P R * Last 7 letters: E R A T I O N \(\rightarrow\) reversed: N O I T A R E * Combined word: F I L O P R N O I T A R E * Positions (1-based): 1: F (consonant), 2: I (vowel), 3: L (consonant), 4: O (vowel), 5: P (consonant), 6: R (consonant), 7: N (consonant), 8: O (vowel), 9: I (vowel), 10: T (consonant), 11: A (vowel), 12: R (consonant), 13: E (vowel). * Odd positions: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13 \(\rightarrow\) Letters: F, L, P, N, I, A, E * Consonants at odd positions: F, L, P, N (Total: 4)

docs.title: Q56. Shadow & Direction Sense
docs.text:
Topic: direction-sense

One morning after sunrise, Mike and John were standing in a lawn with their backs towards each other. Mike's shadow fell exactly towards his right hand side. Which direction was John facing?

Options: - A) East - B) South - C) West - D) North

Correct Answer: D) North

Solution: * In the morning, the sun is in the East, so all shadows fall towards the West. * Mike's shadow fell to his right \(\implies\) Mike's right hand points West. * If a person's right hand points West, they are facing South. * Since Mike and John have their backs to each other, John is facing the opposite direction of South, which is North.

docs.title: Q57. Implicit Assumptions: PVC Pipes
docs.text:
Topic: critical-reasoning

Statement: Use PVC pipes which have life years longer than any other. Assumptions: I. People prefer those pipes which are durable. II. Other pipes are not durable.

Options: - A) Only assumption I is implicit - B) Only assumption II is implicit - C) Neither I nor II is implicit - D) Both I and II are implicit

Correct Answer: A) Only assumption I is implicit Explanation: The advertisement recommends PVC pipes on the basis of durability (longer life years), assuming that durability is preferred by buyers (I is implicit). It does not assume that other pipes are completely non-durable (II is not implicit).

docs.title: Q58. Ranking Puzzle: Rank Difference
docs.text:
Topic: ranking

Six friends - A, B, C, D, E, and F participated in a quiz competition and each scored different marks. The one who scored highest is ranked 1 and so on. * A scored higher than B but lower than C. * D scored higher than E but lower than F. * F scored the highest. * E scored higher than A and C scored less than E.

Question: What will be the difference between the ranks of A and F?

Options: - A) 2 - B) 3 - C) 1 - D) 4

Correct Answer: D) 4

Solution: 1. \(C > A > B\) 2. \(F > D > E\) 3. \(F\) is Rank 1. 4. \(E > A\) 5. \(E > C\) Combining all inequalities: $\(F > D > E > C > A > B\)$ Ranks: F(1), D(2), E(3), C(4), A(5), B(6). Difference between A's rank (5) and F's rank (1) is \(5 - 1 = 4\).

Complete the alpha-numeric series: F6H, N10Q, E?, Q12V

Options: - A) 9H - B) 12H - C) 12I - D) 9I

Correct Answer: D) 9I

Solution: * Last and First Letter Position Difference: * F6H: \(H(8) - F(6) = 2\) * N10Q: \(Q(17) - N(14) = 3\) * E9I: \(I(9) - E(5) = 4\) * Q12V: \(V(22) - Q(17) = 5\) This establishes that the last letter must be I. * Middle Numbers: The sequence of middle numbers is 6, 10, 9, 12, matching the term E9I.

docs.title: Q63. Argument Strength: Demolish buildings
docs.text:
Topic: critical-reasoning

Statement: Should all the houses and shops that are built without permit be demolished? Arguments: I) No, as people would lose their shelter or livelihood. II) Yes, in order to enforce the law and to set an example they must be demolished.

Options: - A) Only argument I is strong - B) Both argument I and argument II are strong - C) Neither argument I nor argument II is strong - D) Only argument II is strong

Correct Answer: B) Both argument I and argument II are strong Explanation: Argument I is strong as it highlights a critical humanitarian issue (loss of shelter/livelihood). Argument II is also strong as it addresses the essential need for legal enforcement and deterrence of illegal activities.

docs.title: Q64. Inside-Outside Circle Puzzle
docs.text:
Topic: number-puzzle

Refer to the double-circle node puzzle. * Top Node: (outside=5, inside=2) * Right Node: (outside=26, inside=5) * Bottom Node: (outside=50, inside=7) * Left Node: (outside=37, inside=?)

Options: - A) 7 - B) 4 - C) 8 - D) 6

Correct Answer: D) 6

Solution: The relation is: \(\text{Outside} = \text{Inside}^2 + 1\). * Top: \(2^2 + 1 = 5\) * Right: \(5^2 + 1 = 26\) * Bottom: \(7^2 + 1 = 50\) * Left: \(?^2 + 1 = 37 \implies ?^2 = 36 \implies ? = 6\).
